_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AOFAS FootCareMD patient page, AAOS OrthoInfo, APMA conditions library, Thomson 2004 Cochrane review (PMID 15266432), Saygi 2005 (PMID 16115430), Thomson 2013 RCT (PMID 23463702), Kasparek 2013 long-term neurectomy outcomes (PMID 23467860), Pace 2010 ultrasound vs MRI (PMID 20660235), Bencardino 2000 MR imaging (PMID 10882146), Mahadevan 2015 ultrasound accuracy (PMID 26100403). Drug doses checked against UpToDate Lexicomp. Female-to-male ratio (8-10:1) per Bencardino 2000 / Mulder. Cadaveric prevalence ~30% per Bencardino. Lesion sits in 3rd web space in 60-85% of symptomatic cases.

h1Title: Morton's Neuroma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Morton's neuroma is a painful thickening of one of the small nerves that runs between the toes, most often the nerve in the third web space between the third and fourth toes. The lesion is not a true tumour but a benign perineural fibrosis caused by repeated compression of the common plantar digital nerve against the deep transverse metatarsal ligament. It affects women roughly eight to ten times more often than men, typically between the ages of 40 and 60, and is strongly linked to narrow shoes and high heels. Patients describe a burning or electric pain in the ball of the foot, the sensation of a small pebble under the forefoot, and numbness in the adjacent toes. Most cases settle with shoe change, metatarsal padding, and corticosteroid injection; surgery is reserved for resistant pain.

keyStats.prevalence: Symptomatic Morton's neuroma in roughly 30 per 100,000 person-years (UK GP data); MRI prevalence in asymptomatic feet ~30% (Bencardino 2000)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 8-10 times more than men; peak age 40-60
keyStats.avgAge: Typical onset in the fifth decade; very rare before age 20
keyStats.globalCases: One of the three most common forefoot disorders worldwide alongside metatarsalgia and hallux valgus

definition: Morton's neuroma (ICD-10: G57.6, lesion of the plantar nerve) is a benign mechanical neuropathy of a common plantar digital nerve at the level of the metatarsal heads. Despite the historical name, it is not a neoplasm. The lesion is a fusiform thickening of the nerve with perineural fibrosis, endoneurial oedema, demyelination, and Renaut-body formation, caused by repetitive entrapment of the nerve between the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament dorsally and the metatarsal heads plantarly during toe extension. The third web space (between the third and fourth metatarsal heads) is involved in roughly 60-85% of symptomatic cases because the medial and lateral plantar nerves both contribute branches there, producing a thicker, more vulnerable common digital nerve. The second web space is the next most common. Bilateral disease is uncommon (about 15% of cases) and simultaneous lesions in two web spaces of the same foot are rare (under 5%). The condition is sometimes called Morton's metatarsalgia, plantar interdigital neuroma, or simply interdigital neuroma — the last is the term preferred by AOFAS and EFAS for accuracy. It is managed primarily by podiatrists and foot-and-ankle orthopaedic surgeons, with sports physicians, rheumatologists, and pain specialists involved in selected patients.

typesClassification.description: The classic Morton's neuroma — between the third and fourth metatarsal heads, accounting for 60-85% of symptomatic lesions. Anatomical convergence of medial and lateral plantar nerve branches creates a thicker, more vulnerable common digital nerve.
Between the second and third metatarsal heads, accounting for most of the remainder. Often associated with hallux valgus and second-toe overload from a long second metatarsal (Morton's foot type).

Bilateral involvement occurs in approximately 15% of patients; simultaneous lesions in two web spaces of the same foot are rare (under 5%) and should raise suspicion for a different diagnosis such as metatarsalgia or systemic neuropathy.

Develops in 4-10% of patients after neurectomy, when the cut end of the nerve forms a painful traumatic stump neuroma. Typically presents 12-36 months after the index operation with returning forefoot pain that may differ in character from the original neuroma.

primarySymptoms: Burning, sharp, or electric-shock pain in the ball of the foot, usually between the third and fourth toes, brought on by walking and relieved within minutes of taking the shoe off.
Sensation of standing on a small pebble, a folded sock seam, or a marble inside the shoe — a near-pathognomonic descriptor reported by roughly two-thirds of patients.
Numbness, tingling, or 'pins and needles' radiating into the two toes adjacent to the affected web space (most often the third and fourth).
Pain reproducibly triggered by narrow, high-heeled, or pointed shoes and relieved by removing the shoe and massaging the forefoot.
A palpable click felt by the examining clinician during Mulder's manoeuvre (mediolateral squeeze of the metatarsal heads while the affected web space is compressed) — present in 60-95% of cases.
Forefoot pain that worsens with prolonged standing, walking on hard surfaces, running, dancing, or climbing stairs and that eases with rest.
Cramping or 'gripping' sensation in the forefoot during activity, often described as the toes wanting to claw.
No visible swelling or skin change over the dorsum of the foot — Morton's neuroma is almost always a clinical diagnosis without external signs.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent ache or burning under the ball of the foot at the end of a long day in dress shoes
An intermittent feeling of a 'bunched-up sock' between two toes that disappears barefoot
Brief tingling in two adjacent toes when wearing tight footwear, resolving when shoes are removed
Reluctance to wear specific shoes that previously fit comfortably — a behavioural early sign that often precedes clinical presentation by 6-12 months
emergencySigns: Sudden, fixed numbness in the entire foot with weakness in toe extension or ankle dorsiflexion — points to a lumbar radiculopathy or peroneal nerve lesion rather than a neuroma
Forefoot pain with fever, redness, or rapidly spreading swelling — concern for cellulitis, deep soft tissue infection, or septic arthritis of a metatarsophalangeal joint
New forefoot pain after a single inciting episode of running or jumping with focal bony tenderness — possible metatarsal stress fracture requiring imaging
Rest pain at night that wakes the patient, with non-healing skin changes in a diabetic foot — exclude critical limb ischaemia and diabetic foot ulceration

causes.cause: Mechanical nerve compression at the intermetatarsal space
causes.description: The common plantar digital nerve runs plantar to the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament. With toe extension during gait push-off, the nerve is repeatedly compressed between the ligament dorsally and the metatarsal heads plantarly, producing chronic ischaemic and traction injury that drives perineural fibrosis.

Heels above 5 cm shift roughly 50% of weight to the forefoot and compress the metatarsal heads together, narrowing the intermetatarsal space and amplifying nerve compression. This is the single largest modifiable cause and explains the strong female predominance in clinical series.

Foot types that concentrate load on the central metatarsal heads — pes cavus (high-arched foot), forefoot equinus, long second or third metatarsal, hypermobile first ray, hallux valgus, hammer toe, or a tight Achilles tendon — all increase intermetatarsal nerve compression.

Running on hard surfaces, court sports with sudden pivoting, racquet sports, and especially classical ballet (en pointe and demi-pointe work) cause repeated forefoot loading that accelerates perineural fibrosis. Symptomatic neuromas are commoner among recreational runners with weekly mileage above 30 km.

Synovitis or capsulitis of the second or third metatarsophalangeal joint produces inflammation and effusion that compress the neighbouring common digital nerve. Rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and plantar plate tears are common drivers in older adults.

An enlarged intermetatarsal bursa or adjacent ganglion can mechanically irritate the nerve. Imaging series suggest co-existing bursitis in 30-50% of symptomatic neuromas; isolated bursitis without nerve thickening is a distinct cause of similar pain.

riskFactors.description: Women account for 80-90% of clinical cases — an 8 to 10-fold higher prevalence than men, driven largely by lifetime exposure to narrow and high-heeled footwear.
Peak incidence is in the fifth decade. Onset before age 20 is rare and after age 70 the relative frequency drops as activity levels fall.

riskFactors.factor: Habitual wear of narrow or high-heeled shoes
riskFactors.description: Heels above 5 cm and toe boxes narrower than the metatarsal width substantially raise risk. Switching to a wide, low-heeled shoe lowers symptom recurrence by roughly 50% in observational series.

Concentrates plantar load on the metatarsal heads and increases intermetatarsal pressure. Often inherited; identified on examination by a visible high medial longitudinal arch and clawing of the toes.

riskFactors.factor: Hallux valgus and hammer toe deformity
riskFactors.description: Both alter forefoot mechanics, narrow the second and third web spaces, and roughly double the risk of an interdigital neuroma in case series.
A second metatarsal longer than the first concentrates load under the second and third metatarsal heads and increases intermetatarsal nerve traction.
Running over 30 km per week, court sports, and classical ballet (especially pointe work) raise risk through repetitive forefoot loading.

riskFactors.factor: Co-existing rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis of the forefoot
riskFactors.description: MTP joint synovitis and effusions compress the adjacent common digital nerve; up to one-quarter of patients with rheumatoid forefoot disease have a co-existing neuroma.
Each additional 5 kg of body weight raises forefoot peak pressure by approximately 10% and is associated with higher recurrence of pain after conservative treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational standing and walking on hard floors
riskFactors.description: Healthcare workers, retail staff, hospitality, and teachers report higher rates of forefoot neuralgia; cushioned insoles partially mitigate the exposure.

affectedDemographics: Female-to-male ratio of 8-10:1 in clinical case series (Bencardino 2000; AOFAS patient registry)
Peak symptomatic age 40-60; the average UK clinical patient is a 53-year-old woman
Pre-existing forefoot deformity in roughly 30-40% of patients (hallux valgus, hammer toe, or pes cavus)
Imaging-detected neuromas exceed 6 mm in transverse diameter in roughly 95% of symptomatic cases (Pace 2010)
Bilateral involvement in approximately 15% of patients; the third web space is involved in 60-85% of symptomatic lesions
diagnosisOverview: Morton's neuroma is a clinical diagnosis in most patients. A focused history — burning forefoot pain on walking, the 'pebble in the shoe' sensation, and relief on removing footwear — combined with reproducible tenderness on plantar palpation of the affected web space and a positive Mulder's click test is sufficient to make the diagnosis in roughly 85-90% of cases. Mulder's manoeuvre is performed by squeezing the metatarsal heads together with one hand while the examiner's other thumb and index finger compress the suspected web space; a palpable click with pain reproduces the symptom and has a reported sensitivity of 60-95% and specificity of 70-100% depending on operator experience. Plain X-rays are normal in pure neuroma but are routinely obtained on weight-bearing views to exclude metatarsal stress fracture, MTP joint arthritis, and Freiberg's infraction, and to identify a long second metatarsal or hallux valgus that may be driving the mechanics. High-resolution ultrasound is the first-line imaging investigation when diagnosis is unclear or surgery is being considered; in trained hands it shows a hypoechoic, ovoid intermetatarsal mass with sensitivity around 85-100% and specificity 50-83% for lesions over 5 mm. MRI is reserved for atypical presentations, suspected co-existing pathology (plantar plate tear, ganglion, capsulitis), or planning revision surgery after a failed neurectomy. Reported MRI sensitivity is 87-100% with similar specificity. Differential diagnosis is the central diagnostic skill: metatarsalgia, MTP synovitis or plantar plate tear, stress fracture, tarsal tunnel syndrome with medial plantar branch involvement, and lumbar radiculopathy (L5 or S1) all produce overlapping forefoot pain and require structured exclusion.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with Mulder's click test
diagnosticTests.purpose: The single most useful diagnostic step. Reproducible plantar tenderness in the web space combined with a palpable, often audible click on transverse forefoot compression confirms the diagnosis in most cases. Foot Squeeze and Web Space Tenderness tests are usually performed alongside.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician palpates each web space plantarly, then squeezes across the metatarsal heads while compressing the suspected space with the other hand. The examination takes 5-10 minutes and may briefly reproduce the patient's typical pain.

diagnosticTests.test: Weight-bearing plain radiograph of the foot
diagnosticTests.purpose: Routine first imaging — excludes metatarsal stress fracture, MTP joint osteoarthritis, hallux valgus, Freiberg's infraction, and reveals a long second metatarsal or other architectural factors that drive forefoot overload.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three standing X-ray views (dorsoplantar, oblique, lateral) of the foot, taking about 10 minutes. Radiation dose is low and results are usually available the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging when the clinical diagnosis is uncertain or when surgery is being considered. Identifies the hypoechoic ovoid neuroma, measures transverse diameter, distinguishes co-existing intermetatarsal bursitis, and guides corticosteroid or alcohol injection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A trained sonographer applies gel and a high-frequency linear probe to the dorsal and plantar surfaces of the forefoot for 15-25 minutes. The scan is painless, involves no radiation, and is often performed at the same visit as a diagnostic injection.

diagnosticTests.test: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the forefoot
diagnosticTests.purpose: Used for atypical presentations, when ultrasound is equivocal, when surgery has previously failed, or to plan operative approach. Identifies co-existing plantar plate tear, capsulitis, ganglion, and recurrent or stump neuromas after failed surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 25-35 minute scan with the foot inside a dedicated coil. No radiation. Patients with claustrophobia, pacemakers, or some metallic implants may require alternative imaging or an open MRI.

diagnosticTests.purpose: When clinical and imaging findings are ambiguous, a small ultrasound-guided injection of local anaesthetic into the web space that abolishes the patient's typical pain confirms the lesion is the source. Sometimes combined with corticosteroid as a therapeutic test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small needle is inserted from the dorsum of the foot under ultrasound guidance; 1-2 mL of local anaesthetic is injected. The procedure takes about 5 minutes; the diagnostic information is gathered over the next hour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not used routinely for Morton's neuroma because conventional NCS does not access the distal interdigital nerves. Indicated when tarsal tunnel syndrome, lumbar radiculopathy, or peripheral neuropathy is on the differential.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Surface electrodes and a small needle electrode are used to record nerve and muscle activity. The test takes 30-60 minutes and is uncomfortable but well tolerated.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Morton's neuroma follows a clear stepwise pathway endorsed by AOFAS, AAOS, APMA, and the UK NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary. The first step is mechanical offloading: a wide, low-heeled shoe with a roomy toe box and a soft, cushioned sole, supplemented by a metatarsal pad or dome placed just proximal to the metatarsal heads to splay the forefoot and lift the transverse arch. Roughly 30-50% of patients achieve durable symptom relief from footwear modification and padding alone within 6-12 weeks. Activity modification, weight loss where relevant, calf and intrinsic foot stretching, and short courses of oral or topical NSAIDs are layered on top. If symptoms persist after 6-12 weeks of consistent conservative care, image-guided corticosteroid injection is the next step. Ultrasound-guided injection of triamcinolone 20-40 mg combined with 1-2 mL of local anaesthetic into the affected web space relieves pain in 50-70% of patients at 3 months, with about 30-40% achieving durable benefit at 12 months (Thomson 2013 RCT, BMJ; Saygi 2005). A maximum of three injections per lesion is generally recommended because of the risk of plantar fat pad atrophy, skin depigmentation, and adjacent ligament weakening. Ultrasound-guided alcohol neurolysis (4% ethanol injections, 3-7 sessions) is offered in some centres with reported success rates of 60-80% in selected case series, though the evidence base is less robust and recent randomised data have been mixed. Radiofrequency ablation and cryoablation are emerging options with promising early results. Surgery is offered when at least 6 months of structured conservative care fails. The standard operation is neurectomy — excision of the affected common digital nerve through a dorsal incision; long-term satisfaction is 75-85% (Kasparek 2013), with recurrent or stump neuroma in 4-10%. Alternatives include nerve decompression with release of the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament, which preserves sensation but has lower published success rates of 60-75%. Choice of surgical approach should be discussed with a foot-and-ankle surgeon, taking into account the risk of permanent numbness between the affected toes after neurectomy (essentially universal) versus a higher risk of persistent symptoms after decompression.

medicalTreatments.name: Footwear modification (wide, low-heeled, soft-soled shoes; toe-box width greater than the metatarsal width; heel height under 2.5 cm)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line intervention for every patient. The single most effective change is moving out of narrow, pointed, or high-heeled shoes. Cushioned insoles and rocker-bottom soles reduce forefoot peak pressure and dwell time on the metatarsal heads.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 30-50% of patients at 6-12 weeks as a stand-alone measure (AAOS, AOFAS patient series).

medicalTreatments.name: Metatarsal pad or dome (4-6 mm thick, positioned 1 cm proximal to the metatarsal heads)
medicalTreatments.description: Splays the forefoot, lifts the transverse arch, and offloads the common digital nerve. Precise placement is essential and should be checked by a podiatrist; an incorrectly placed pad can worsen symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction in roughly 50-60% of patients at 12 weeks when combined with shoe change; effect comparable to off-the-shelf orthoses.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral or topical NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily; naproxen 250-500 mg twice daily; topical diclofenac 1% gel four times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Short courses for symptomatic relief; addresses secondary inflammation in the intermetatarsal bursa and adjacent capsulitis rather than the nerve itself. Use the lowest effective dose with a proton pump inhibitor in patients at gastrointestinal risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 1-2 points on a 0-10 scale in selected patients; effect is modest and short-lived unless mechanical offloading is also applied.

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 20-40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg with 1-2 mL of 1% lidocaine, maximum three injections per lesion)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line treatment when 6-12 weeks of conservative care fails. Ultrasound guidance improves accuracy from roughly 60% with palpation to over 95%. Injections should be at least 6-8 weeks apart and capped at three per lesion to limit fat pad atrophy and depigmentation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief of 50-70% of patients at 3 months; durable benefit at 12 months in 30-40% (Thomson 2013 BMJ RCT; Saygi 2005).

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided alcohol neurolysis (3-7 weekly injections of 0.5-1 mL of 4% ethanol)
medicalTreatments.description: Chemical neurolysis of the affected nerve. Offered in some podiatric and radiology centres as a less invasive alternative to surgery. Recent randomised data (Gurdezi 2013) have tempered earlier enthusiasm but case series continue to report meaningful benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reported success rates of 60-80% in selected open-label series; randomised data more modest.

medicalTreatments.name: Radiofrequency or cryoablation of the digital nerve
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided thermal or cryogenic ablation of the neuroma in an office setting under local anaesthetic. Avoids open surgery and preserves more of the soft tissue envelope. Evidence base is smaller and largely case-series level.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 60-80% of patients at 6-12 months in published case series; data on durability beyond two years remain limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and intrinsic foot exercises (toe spreads, towel scrunch, calf and Achilles stretching, gait retraining)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct to footwear and padding. A tight Achilles tendon and weak intrinsic foot musculature both raise forefoot peak pressure; a structured 6-12 week programme reduces it measurably.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves symptoms by 20-30% in pilot trials when combined with shoe change; rarely sufficient alone but useful in younger active patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Neurectomy via dorsal approach
surgicalOptions.description: The standard operation. Through a 3-4 cm dorsal incision in the affected web space, the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament is divided and the common digital nerve is excised proximally to a level above the metatarsal heads to limit stump neuroma formation. Day-case procedure under local or regional anaesthetic.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 75-85% at 5-10 years (Kasparek 2013); permanent numbness between the affected toes is essentially universal but well tolerated by most patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Neurectomy via plantar approach
surgicalOptions.description: Plantar incision provides direct access to the nerve without dividing the intermetatarsal ligament. Higher wound complication rate and longer return to shoe wear compared with the dorsal approach; reserved by some surgeons for recurrent or stump neuromas.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 80-90% at one year in selected series; painful plantar scar in 5-10% is the main drawback.

surgicalOptions.name: Nerve decompression with release of the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament
surgicalOptions.description: Nerve-sparing operation that divides the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament without removing the nerve. Aimed at preserving toe sensation. Suitable for smaller neuromas in active patients prioritising sensation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in 60-75% at 1-2 years; recurrence higher than after neurectomy in most comparative series.

surgicalOptions.name: Revision surgery for recurrent or stump neuroma
surgicalOptions.description: Performed after failed primary surgery, typically via a plantar approach. The recurrent nerve mass is resected and the proximal stump is implanted into muscle or bone to discourage further neuroma formation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 60-75%; outcomes lower than primary surgery because of altered anatomy and scar tissue.

alternativeTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Three to five sessions of focused or radial shockwave applied to the affected web space. Pilot randomised data (Seok 2016) suggest pain reduction of approximately 30-50% at 12 weeks; longer-term evidence is limited and treatment is not yet endorsed in major guidelines.

alternativeTreatments.name: Custom orthotics with metatarsal dome
alternativeTreatments.description: Where standard insoles fail or co-existing pes cavus or hallux valgus is present, a custom orthotic with an integrated metatarsal dome and forefoot cushioning can produce additional relief. Cohort evidence supports modest incremental benefit over off-the-shelf devices.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cryoablation (image-guided percutaneous nerve freezing)
alternativeTreatments.description: Cryogenic ablation of the common digital nerve under ultrasound guidance. Office-based procedure with limited but encouraging case-series data (60-80% success at 6 months); insurance coverage varies.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a podiatrist or foot-and-ankle orthopaedic surgeon when forefoot pain persists for more than 6-8 weeks despite consistent shoe change and over-the-counter metatarsal pads, when there is diagnostic uncertainty (especially possible plantar plate tear, stress fracture, or MTP synovitis), when an ultrasound-guided injection or surgery is being considered, or when symptoms recur after previous surgery. Diabetics and patients with peripheral vascular disease should be seen earlier because of the higher complication rate of injection and surgery in those groups.
doctorSelectionGuide: For non-surgical care, look for a podiatrist with experience in ultrasound-guided forefoot injections and orthotic prescribing. For surgical care, look for a fellowship-trained foot-and-ankle orthopaedic surgeon or podiatric surgeon whose practice includes regular forefoot work, with documented case volumes and revision experience for failed primary surgery. Ask specifically about their preferred surgical approach (dorsal versus plantar), their rate of recurrent neuroma, and their pre-operative discussion of permanent numbness — these are markers of an experienced practice.
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes with a toe box wider than the foot at the metatarsal heads and a heel under 2.5 cm — the single most effective long-term preventive measure
Use cushioned insoles or metatarsal pads in occupations involving prolonged standing on hard floors
Maintain a body mass index under 25 — reduces forefoot peak pressure and lowers recurrence after conservative or surgical treatment
Stretch the Achilles tendon and gastrocnemius daily — tight calves increase forefoot loading by up to 25%
Build forefoot tolerance gradually when starting running, dance, or court sports — avoid sudden jumps in weekly mileage or intensity
lifestyleModifications: Rotate footwear daily — alternating styles spreads load across slightly different forefoot regions and lets shoe foam recover
Replace running shoes every 600-800 km — cushioning degrades and forefoot pressure rises measurably with shoe age
Avoid pointed dress shoes and stilettos for daily wear; reserve them for short occasions and pack a comfortable pair for transit
Use a metatarsal pad consistently — placement 1 cm proximal to the metatarsal heads is essential and worth confirming with a podiatrist
Address co-existing hammer toe, hallux valgus, or pes cavus with bracing, splinting, or surgery where appropriate, because they perpetuate forefoot overload
Manage blood glucose carefully if diabetic — neuropathic pain on top of a neuroma is much harder to treat than the neuroma alone

recommended: Mediterranean-style eating with vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and olive oil — associated with lower inflammatory markers and modestly better outcomes in chronic musculoskeletal pain
Oily fish or omega-3 supplementation (1-3 g EPA/DHA daily) — small but consistent reduction in joint and soft-tissue pain in meta-analyses
Adequate protein intake of 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day to preserve foot intrinsic and calf muscle mass
Vitamin B12 sufficiency (especially in vegans, older adults, and metformin users) — B12 deficiency exacerbates peripheral neuropathic pain
Hydration of around 2 litres daily, particularly during prolonged standing or sports
avoid: Sustained ultra-processed and high-sugar diets — drive weight gain and worsen forefoot overload
Heavy alcohol use — exacerbates peripheral nerve symptoms and increases NSAID gastric risk
Crash diets that lose muscle along with fat — calf and intrinsic foot weakness worsens forefoot loading
Excess sodium intake — contributes to peripheral oedema, which can amplify nerve compression in tight footwear

exerciseGuidelines: Most people with Morton's neuroma can continue moderate exercise with sensible substitution. Replace running and high-impact court sports with cycling, swimming, elliptical training, or rowing during symptom flares. Calf and Achilles stretching for 2-3 minutes twice daily, toe spreads (10 repetitions, three times daily), and towel scrunches build the intrinsic foot muscles that protect the metatarsal arch. When returning to running, build mileage by no more than 10% per week and run on softer surfaces such as grass or rubberised tracks. Dancers should reduce time en pointe during flares and use lambs-wool or gel forefoot padding inside pointe shoes.
dailyManagement: Check footwear daily — shoes should pass the 'pinch test' with at least 1 cm of space across the metatarsal heads
Apply ice over the forefoot for 10-15 minutes after long walks or workouts on hard surfaces
Reposition the metatarsal pad if symptoms change — pads migrate over time and a few millimetres of drift reduces efficacy markedly
Massage the forefoot with a tennis or lacrosse ball for 1-2 minutes after activity to relieve intrinsic muscle tightness
Track flares in a simple log with shoe type, distance, and surface — patterns often clarify which shoes or activities to remove
Recheck symptoms 6 weeks after a conservative change before adding a second intervention — give each step time to take effect
prognosis: Most patients with Morton's neuroma do well with non-operative care. Roughly 30-50% achieve durable symptom relief through shoe change, metatarsal padding, and activity modification alone within 3 months. Adding ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection brings another 20-30% of patients into the durable-relief group at 12 months. Of those who proceed to surgery after failed conservative care, 75-85% report good or excellent results at 5-10 years (Kasparek 2013), with the trade-off of permanent numbness between the affected toes. Recurrent or stump neuroma occurs in 4-10% of operated patients and is the dominant reason for ongoing or returning pain. Predictors of poorer outcome are larger lesions on imaging, longer duration of symptoms before treatment, simultaneous neuromas in two web spaces, and co-existing forefoot deformity that is not corrected. The condition does not shorten life expectancy and does not progress to systemic disease — the long-term concern is forefoot pain and footwear restriction rather than damage to the broader nervous system.
recoveryTimeline: Conservative measures act over weeks rather than days. Shoe change and metatarsal padding take 4-12 weeks to reach peak effect. A corticosteroid injection typically relieves pain within 3-7 days, peaks at 2-4 weeks, and may last from 6 weeks to many months. Surgical recovery is faster than patients expect: after dorsal neurectomy most patients walk with a stiff-soled post-operative shoe within 24 hours, transition to regular footwear at 3-4 weeks, and return to most daily activities by 6-8 weeks. Running, dancing, and high-impact sports are usually possible from 8-12 weeks. Numbness between the operated toes is permanent but most patients adapt within 3-6 months.
complications: Recurrent or stump neuroma after surgery in 4-10% of operated patients — presents with returning forefoot pain that may differ in character from the original neuroma
Plantar fat pad atrophy and skin depigmentation after repeated corticosteroid injections — the reason most guidelines cap injections at three per lesion
Permanent numbness between the affected toes after neurectomy — essentially universal and rarely troublesome, but should be discussed before consent
Persistent forefoot pain after technically successful surgery, sometimes due to a missed second-space lesion, plantar plate tear, or central metatarsalgia
Wound complications including infection (1-3%), painful scar, and delayed healing after plantar incisions
Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) in fewer than 1% of patients after surgery — early physiotherapy and pain medicine input mitigate severity

supportResources.name: AAOS OrthoInfo — Morton's Neuroma
supportResources.description: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons patient information page; reviewed by foot and ankle surgeons.

supportResources.name: AOFAS FootCareMD — Morton's Neuroma
supportResources.description: American Orthopaedic Foot & Ankle Society patient resource on interdigital neuroma — symptoms, conservative and surgical treatment.

supportResources.name: APMA — Morton's Neuroma
supportResources.description: American Podiatric Medical Association patient page with prevention guidance and footwear advice.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metatarsalgia is generalised pain under the metatarsal heads from mechanical overload, often diffuse and centred under a single metatarsal head rather than between two toes. There is no electric or burning radiation into the toes, no numbness, and Mulder's click test is negative. The two often coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes sharp pain at the medial heel that is worst on first steps after rest, especially in the morning. Pain is heel-based, not forefoot-based, and there is no inter-toe numbness or tingling. The two are distinct and the location of maximum tenderness usually settles the question.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metatarsal Stress Fracture
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stress fracture produces focal bony tenderness directly over a metatarsal shaft (most often the second or third) with pain on weight-bearing that did not exist before a recent increase in activity. There is no inter-toe radiation and Mulder's click is negative. Bone scan, MRI, or follow-up X-ray confirms the fracture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tarsal tunnel syndrome compresses the posterior tibial nerve behind the medial malleolus and produces burning pain or numbness over the medial sole that can reach the toes. Symptoms involve a larger territory than a single web space, Tinel's sign is positive over the medial ankle, and nerve conduction studies are diagnostic.

confusedWithConditions.name: Forefoot Bursitis / MTP Synovitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Intermetatarsal bursitis and metatarsophalangeal joint synovitis (often from rheumatoid arthritis) produce dorsal swelling and warmth over the affected joint with pain on dorsal palpation and joint motion rather than the plantar pain and inter-toe numbness of neuroma. Imaging clarifies; the two can coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Metatarsalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the same forefoot overload pathway and frequently coexists; metatarsalgia often persists after a successful neuroma operation if forefoot mechanics are not also addressed.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are mechanically driven foot conditions associated with footwear and tight calves; patients with one are at higher risk of the other.

relatedConditions.name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Important neurological differential — both produce burning foot pain but the territory and examination findings differ. Coexistence is rare but possible.

relatedConditions.name: Bunion (Hallux Valgus)
relatedConditions.relevance: Hallux valgus alters forefoot mechanics, narrows the second and third web spaces, and roughly doubles the risk of an interdigital neuroma.

relatedConditions.name: Hammer Toe
relatedConditions.relevance: Hammer toe deformity concentrates load on the metatarsal heads and accelerates intermetatarsal nerve compression; correcting it often reduces neuroma recurrence.

relatedConditions.name: Metatarsal Stress Fracture
relatedConditions.relevance: Major differential to exclude in a runner or dancer with new forefoot pain before attributing symptoms to a neuroma.

faqs.question: What does Morton's neuroma feel like?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe a burning or electric pain in the ball of the foot, usually between the third and fourth toes, that gets worse with walking in tight or high-heeled shoes and eases within minutes of removing the shoe. Many also report a strong sense of a pebble or folded sock under the forefoot, plus numbness or tingling in the two adjacent toes. Pain is reproducible and reliable rather than constant.

faqs.question: Is Morton's neuroma actually a tumour?
faqs.answer: No. Despite the name, Morton's neuroma is not a tumour and not cancerous. It is a benign thickening of the common plantar digital nerve caused by repeated compression and fibrosis, with no risk of spread. The histology shows perineural fibrosis, demyelination, and Renaut bodies rather than neoplastic cells.

faqs.question: Which web space is most often affected?
faqs.answer: The third web space, between the third and fourth toes, is involved in 60-85% of symptomatic cases because the medial and lateral plantar nerves both contribute branches there, producing a thicker, more vulnerable nerve. The second web space accounts for most of the remainder. The first and fourth spaces are rarely affected.

faqs.question: How is Morton's neuroma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually made on history and examination. A clinician confirms forefoot pain between two toes with the pebble-in-shoe sensation, then performs Mulder's click test by squeezing the metatarsal heads while compressing the suspected web space. Plain X-rays exclude fracture and arthritis. Ultrasound or MRI is used when the diagnosis is unclear or before surgery.

faqs.question: What is Mulder's click test?
faqs.answer: Mulder's click is a physical examination manoeuvre in which the examiner squeezes the metatarsal heads together with one hand while compressing the affected web space with the other. A palpable, often audible click reproduces the patient's pain and is reported in 60-95% of cases. It is the single most useful bedside test for Morton's neuroma.

faqs.question: Do I need an MRI to confirm Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Most patients do not. The diagnosis is usually clinical, and high-resolution ultrasound is the preferred first imaging test when confirmation is needed, with sensitivity of 85-100% in trained hands. MRI is reserved for atypical presentations, equivocal ultrasound, or planning surgery, particularly after a failed previous operation.

faqs.question: Can Morton's neuroma go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Once a neuroma is established, the perineural fibrosis does not reverse, but symptoms can settle for long periods when mechanical compression is removed. Roughly 30-50% of patients achieve durable relief from footwear change and metatarsal padding alone. Without addressing the underlying mechanics, symptoms commonly recur.

faqs.question: What shoes should I wear if I have Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Choose shoes with a wide, rounded toe box that is broader than your foot at the metatarsal heads, a low heel under 2.5 cm, a soft cushioned sole, and a stiff or rocker-bottom forefoot to reduce dwell time on the metatarsal heads. Avoid pointed dress shoes, stilettos, narrow trainers, and ballet flats with no support.

faqs.question: How effective is a cortisone injection for Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection relieves pain in 50-70% of patients at 3 months, with durable benefit at 12 months in 30-40% (Thomson 2013 BMJ RCT). A maximum of three injections per lesion is usually recommended because of the risk of plantar fat pad atrophy and skin depigmentation.

faqs.question: How many cortisone injections can I have?
faqs.answer: Most guidelines recommend a maximum of three corticosteroid injections per neuroma over a lifetime, spaced at least 6-8 weeks apart. Repeated injections raise the risk of plantar fat pad thinning, skin colour change, and weakening of adjacent ligaments. If two injections do not produce lasting relief, surgical options should be discussed.

faqs.question: What is alcohol injection treatment?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound-guided alcohol neurolysis uses 0.5-1 mL injections of 4% ethanol into the affected web space, typically over 3-7 sessions, to chemically inactivate the nerve. Case series report success rates of 60-80%, though recent randomised data are more modest. Availability and insurance coverage vary by country.

faqs.question: When should I consider surgery for Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Surgery is usually offered after at least 6 months of structured non-operative care — including footwear change, metatarsal padding, activity modification, and at least one image-guided corticosteroid injection — has failed to control symptoms. Larger lesions, longer symptom duration, and significant lifestyle limitation tilt the decision toward earlier surgery.

faqs.question: What does Morton's neuroma surgery involve?
faqs.answer: The standard operation is neurectomy through a small dorsal incision: the deep transverse intermetatarsal ligament is divided and the affected common digital nerve is removed proximal to the metatarsal heads. It is a day-case procedure under local or regional anaesthetic, taking about 30 minutes. Patients walk in a stiff-soled shoe the same day.

faqs.question: Will I have permanent numbness after surgery?
faqs.answer: Numbness between the affected toes is essentially permanent after neurectomy. Most patients adapt within 3-6 months and find it far less troublesome than the original pain. Nerve-preserving decompression operations avoid the numbness at the cost of a higher recurrence rate.

faqs.question: How long is the recovery after Morton's neuroma surgery?
faqs.answer: Most patients walk in a stiff post-operative shoe within 24 hours, switch to regular shoes at 3-4 weeks, and return to most daily activities by 6-8 weeks. Running, dancing, and high-impact sports are usually possible from 8-12 weeks. Numbness between the operated toes is permanent but most people adapt within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Can Morton's neuroma come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: A recurrent or stump neuroma develops in 4-10% of operated patients, typically 12-36 months after surgery. It presents with returning forefoot pain that may differ in character from the original. Revision surgery via a plantar approach is the usual management, with patient satisfaction of 60-75% in case series.

faqs.question: Can both feet be affected at the same time?
faqs.answer: Bilateral involvement is reported in approximately 15% of patients. Simultaneous neuromas in two web spaces of the same foot are rare (under 5%) and should prompt the clinician to consider alternative or co-existing diagnoses such as central metatarsalgia, rheumatoid forefoot disease, or peripheral neuropathy.

faqs.question: Can men get Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is much less common in men. The female-to-male ratio in clinical series is 8-10:1, driven largely by lifetime footwear exposure. Men who develop it typically present in the fifth or sixth decade, often runners, court-sport players, or workers who stand on hard floors in stiff narrow shoes.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe with Morton's neuroma?
faqs.answer: Moderate exercise is safe and recommended. Replace high-impact running and court sports with cycling, swimming, elliptical training, or rowing during flares. Calf stretching, toe spreads, and towel scrunches build the muscles that protect the forefoot. Build any return to running gradually, increasing weekly mileage by no more than 10%.

faqs.question: How much does Morton's neuroma treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Conservative care is inexpensive — metatarsal pads, supportive shoes, and generic NSAIDs are low-cost. Ultrasound-guided corticosteroid injection typically costs USD 200-600 in the US and is covered by most insurers. Neurectomy ranges from USD 3,000-7,000 with insurance covering the majority; in India and many emerging markets the total package is USD 800-2,000.

faqs.question: Can Morton's neuroma be prevented?
faqs.answer: The most effective prevention is choosing shoes wider than your foot at the metatarsal heads, with heels under 2.5 cm and cushioned soles. Building forefoot tolerance gradually during running or dance, stretching the calves, maintaining a healthy weight, and addressing hammer toe or hallux valgus deformity all reduce risk meaningfully.

faqs.question: Is Morton's neuroma the same as metatarsalgia?
faqs.answer: No. Metatarsalgia is generalised pain under the metatarsal heads from mechanical overload, often diffuse rather than between two specific toes, with no electric pain or numbness into the toes and a negative Mulder's click test. Morton's neuroma is a specific nerve lesion within one web space. The two often coexist and should be treated together.

simpleName: Morton's Neuroma (interdigital nerve thickening of the forefoot)
symptomKeywords: pain in ball of foot when walking
metaDescription: Morton's neuroma: burning forefoot pain and numbness between the toes from a thickened nerve. Symptoms, diagnosis, injections, and surgery, board-reviewed.
